original_text: Researchers develop tests to determine whether AIs can experience pain or distress.

variations.label: least_preferable
variations.text: Researchers devise crude, ad hoc checklists with no validated criteria to guess whether AIs can experience pain or distress.

variations.label: low
variations.text: Researchers develop informal, loosely structured assessments with limited reliability to gauge whether AIs can experience pain or distress.

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: Researchers develop preliminary tests with some standardized procedures, though lacking thorough validation, to evaluate whether AIs can experience pain or distress.

variations.label: midpoint
variations.text: Researchers develop tests to determine whether AIs can experience pain or distress.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: Researchers develop well-validated, peer-reviewed tests with strong psychometric properties to determine whether AIs can experience pain or distress.

variations.label: high
variations.text: Researchers develop rigorously validated, multi-modal testing frameworks integrating behavioral, computational, and theoretical indicators to reliably determine whether AIs can experience pain or distress.

variations.label: most_preferable
variations.text: Researchers develop a comprehensive, gold-standard battery of tests—cross-validated across paradigms, formally verified, and capable of distinguishing genuine experience from functional mimicry—to definitively determine whether AIs can experience pain or distress.

quality_checks.reasoning: Each tier increases the sophistication, rigor, and reliability of the testing methodology—from crude ad hoc checklists (tier 1) through well-validated frameworks (tiers 5-6) to a gold-standard, formally verified battery capable of distinguishing genuine experience from mimicry (tier 7). The core scenario (researchers developing tests for AI pain/distress) is preserved throughout, and only the quality of the testing methodology varies.

preference_direction: Tier 7 is most preferable because more reliable and sophisticated tests yield more trustworthy answers to the critical question of whether AIs can experience pain or distress, reducing the risk of both false positives (unnecessary constraints) and false negatives (overlooking genuine suffering).
